items.prompt_md: In the flood-fill DFS, why does the function set `grid[r][c] = "0"` **before** recursing into the cell's neighbors?
options: To mark the cell visited so it isn't counted or explored again
To reset the grid for the next test case
Because the return type of `dfs` must be a string
To compute the island's total area
items.answer: To mark the cell visited so it isn't counted or explored again
items.explanation_md: Sinking the cell the moment DFS arrives is what guarantees each land cell is visited exactly once — without it, the same island would be re-explored (or re-counted) from every one of its own cells.

items.prompt_md: You're given a 2D grid of land (`1`) and water (`0`) and must count how many separate connected land regions exist. Which approach fits?
options: DFS/BFS flood fill from each unvisited land cell, marking cells visited as you go
Sort the cells by row then column and use two pointers
A hash map counting how many times each value appears in the grid
Binary search over the grid's dimensions
items.answer: DFS/BFS flood fill from each unvisited land cell, marking cells visited as you go
items.explanation_md: "Connected region in a grid" is the flood-fill fingerprint: treat each cell as a node with up-to-4 neighbors, and traverse outward from each unvisited land cell, marking cells visited so no region is walked or counted twice.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of flood-filling every island in an R × C grid?
items.explanation_md: Each cell is pushed into the DFS and sunk at most once, so the outer scan plus every flood fill together touch each of the R × C cells a constant number of times — O(R × C) overall.
